units.title: The Age of Exploration and Colonial America
essential_questions: What motivated European exploration and colonization?
How did colonization impact Indigenous peoples and Africans?
units.description: Students examine the causes and consequences of European exploration, the development of colonial societies, and the transatlantic slave trade.

units.title: Road to Revolution
essential_questions: Was the American Revolution inevitable?
How do Enlightenment ideas justify revolution?
units.description: Students trace colonial grievances from the Stamp Act through the Boston Tea Party and analyze how Enlightenment philosophy gave colonists a language for resistance.

units.title: Founding a Nation: The Constitution
essential_questions: How did the Founders balance liberty and order?
Whose voices were left out of the founding?
units.description: Students analyze the Articles of Confederation, Constitutional Convention debates, and the Bill of Rights, with attention to whose interests the Constitution protected and whose it ignored.

units.title: The Early Republic and Expansion
essential_questions: How did Manifest Destiny justify and mask violence?
What did 'freedom' mean in the early republic?
units.description: Students examine westward expansion, Indian Removal, and the economic and political tensions driving the nation toward the Civil War.

units.title: Slavery, Abolition, and the Road to Civil War
essential_questions: How did slavery shape every aspect of American society?
Was the Civil War primarily about slavery or states' rights?
units.description: Students examine the institution of slavery, the abolitionist movement, the causes of the Civil War, and key turning points of the conflict.

units.title: Reconstruction
essential_questions: What did Reconstruction accomplish — and why did it fail?
How do we measure the success of a social change movement?
units.description: Students analyze the Reconstruction Amendments, the gains of the Reconstruction era, and the violent rollback of Black political power through Black Codes, the KKK, and Jim Crow.

units.title: Industrialization and the Gilded Age
essential_questions: Who benefited from industrialization — and who paid the price?
How do workers gain power?
units.description: Students examine industrial capitalism, immigration, urbanization, the labor movement, and reform-era responses to inequality.

units.title: The Progressive Era and WWI
essential_questions: Can ordinary citizens change unjust systems?
What obligations does a democracy have in times of global conflict?
units.description: Students study Progressive Era reforms — women's suffrage, trust-busting, child labor laws — and American entry into World War I.

units.title: Roaring 20s, Great Depression, New Deal
essential_questions: What causes economic collapse — and who is responsible for recovery?
What is the proper role of government in the economy?
units.description: Students analyze the economic boom of the 1920s, the Great Depression's causes and human impact, and FDR's New Deal as a contested government response.

units.title: World War II
essential_questions: How did the U.S. balance its democratic values with wartime decisions?
What obligations do bystanders have when witnessing injustice?
units.description: Students study the causes of WWII, U.S. entry, key turning points, and moral dilemmas including Japanese American internment, the Holocaust, and the atomic bomb.

big_ideas.idea: The Tension Between Liberty and Equality
Who Gets to Count as American?

assessment_calendar.title: Mid-Year Benchmark: Colonial America through Civil War
